audition.text:
How does the brain construct our subjective experience of sounds, and what determines our confidence in what we hear? These fundamental questions about auditory perception have led us to explore how different states of consciousness shape our ability to detect and interpret acoustic information.

In our [Confidence in Auditory Perception](#hearing) project, we are investigating the relationship between objective auditory performance and subjective confidence across varying states of awareness. Using psychoacoustic testing paradigms, we measure how individuals detect subtle auditory signals while simultaneously reporting their confidence in these perceptual decisions. This approach allows us to dissociate the sensory processing of sounds from the metacognitive evaluation of that processing—revealing how factors beyond the physical properties of sounds influence what we think we hear.

Our work extends beyond simple detection tasks to examine how contextual factors and internal states modulate auditory perception. By studying these processes in humans, we can leverage introspective reports to understand not just whether a sound was detected, but how certain people feel about their perceptual experiences. This research promises to illuminate the mechanisms through which the brain transforms acoustic waves into the rich auditory world we consciously experience, while providing insights into how confidence shapes perceptual decision-making more broadly.

biophysics.text:
How do the molecular dynamics of ion channels and synapses give rise to the computational capabilities of neural circuits? We explored this fundamental question through detailed biophysical modeling combined with experimental validation, seeking to bridge the gap between molecular mechanisms and neural function.

Our early work focused on developing [kinetic models](#kinetic-models) that capture how ion channels transition between conformational states to shape synaptic transmission. We created mathematical frameworks that [unify the description of voltage-gated channels, synaptic receptors, and neuromodulation](https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00961734), providing [efficient computational methods](https://doi.org/10.1162/neco.1994.6.1.14) for simulating these processes. This theoretical foundation enabled us to investigate how [dendritic structure influences neuronal firing patterns](https://doi.org/10.1038/382363a0) and to demonstrate that cortical neurons can generate [remarkably reliable spike timing](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.7770778) despite the apparent randomness of synaptic inputs—a finding that challenged prevailing views about neural coding.

Building on these computational insights, we developed experimental approaches to directly observe synaptic dynamics in living tissue. Our [synaptic plasticity studies](#synaptic-plasticity) pioneered the use of two-photon microscopy to visualize calcium dynamics at individual dendritic spines in hippocampal slices, revealing how [Hebbian learning principles](#hippo-dendrites) sculpt synaptic organization. We applied similar approaches to understand [olfactory bulb circuitry](#olfactory-bulb), where we investigated how dendrodendritic synapses between mitral and granule cells create lateral inhibition patterns that shape odor processing. Through this integrated approach—combining rigorous biophysical modeling with cutting-edge imaging and electrophysiology—we established how the molecular machinery of neurons enables the precise spatiotemporal dynamics underlying neural computation.

consciousness.text:
What is consciousness, and how can neuroscience illuminate this most fundamental aspect of experience? We began by confronting the challenge of [first-person subjective experience](#consciousness-philosophy)—the ineffable "what it's like" quality that defines consciousness. Rather than dismissing subjective experience as beyond scientific reach, we developed theoretical frameworks that bridge phenomenology and neuroscience, arguing that [consciousness cannot be fully understood without considering the first-person perspective](https://doi.org/10.1038/78751). This work revealed how [first-person experience pervades cognitive science](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11229-019-02136-6), even in ostensibly objective domains.

Our exploration of consciousness expanded through investigations of [voluntary action and free will](#voluntary-action). Using electrophysiology in rats, we examined how neural circuits generate self-initiated actions, revealing the temporal dynamics of decision-making in the brain. These [empirical findings raised profound questions about agency and determinism](https://doi.org/10.1101/sqb.2014.79.024810), showing how neuroscience can inform age-old philosophical debates. We also recognized that understanding consciousness requires new methodological approaches, leading us to develop frameworks for [analyzing the vast behavioral datasets](#big-behavioral-data) generated by modern neuroscience. These tools allow us to capture the richness of natural behavior that traditional reductionist approaches might miss, providing [expanded perspectives on cognition across humans, animals, and machines](https://doi.org/10.1101/037192).

Currently, we are pursuing three complementary approaches to consciousness. In our [LSD studies](#mouse-lsd), we use psychedelics as a tool to perturb consciousness systematically, recording from thousands of neurons simultaneously with Neuropixels probes to understand how altered states emerge from changes in neural population dynamics. Our [social flavor perception](#sharing) project examines how consciousness is shaped by social context, investigating how sharing gustatory experiences transforms individual perception through group dynamics. Finally, our work on [virtual reality in perioperative settings](#vr-surgery) explores practical applications of consciousness research, examining whether immersive experiences can modulate anxiety and pain perception in surgical patients. Together, these projects reflect our commitment to understanding consciousness through multiple lenses—from molecular perturbations to social interactions—while maintaining the centrality of subjective experience in our scientific inquiry.

decision.text:
How do animals make decisions in the face of uncertainty, and what gives them confidence in their choices? This fundamental question has driven our research for two decades, leading us from the neural circuits of sensory decision-making to computational theories of confidence that span species and brain systems.

Our journey began with [olfactory decision-making](#olfactory-decisions) in rats, where we discovered that these animals can make remarkably accurate odor-based choices in just a few hundred milliseconds. Through electrophysiological recordings, we found that neurons in the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) don't just encode the decision itself—they also signal the animal's confidence in that choice. This led us to investigate [how OFC activity guides confidence-based behaviors](#ofc-confidence), revealing that these neurons predict how long an animal will wait for reward based on their certainty about being correct. When we disrupted OFC function, rats lost the ability to adjust their waiting time according to decision difficulty, demonstrating that this region is [required for optimal confidence-guided behavior](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2014.08.039).

Building on these findings, we developed a [computational framework](https://doi.org/10.1098/rstb.2012.0037) that treats confidence as a Bayesian probability—the brain's estimate of being correct given the available evidence. This theoretical approach helped explain our empirical observations across multiple brain regions, from [sensorimotor decisions in the superior colliculus](#superior-colliculus) to abstract choices involving the basal ganglia. Our [computational work](#computational-decisions) revealed that confidence isn't just an epiphenomenon but serves critical functions: it guides learning rates, controls information-seeking behavior, and enables optimal decision-making under uncertainty. Today, through the [International Brain Laboratory](#ibl), we're mapping how confidence signals emerge across the entire brain during perceptual decisions, while our [facial expression decoding](#face-flipping-decoding) project explores how these internal confidence states manifest in spontaneous behaviors. From [neural mechanisms](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature07200) to [computational principles](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2015.09.039) to behavioral readouts, we continue to unravel how brains not only make decisions but also know when they're right.

dendritic.text:
How do the intricate branches of dendrites transform synaptic inputs into neural computations? This fundamental question drove our early investigations into dendritic function, beginning with computational models of [hippocampal neurons](#hippo-dendrites) that revealed how the spatial arrangement of synapses along dendritic trees shapes learning and memory. We discovered that [dendritic structure profoundly influences firing patterns](https://doi.org/10.1038/382363a0), with the [electrotonic architecture of hippocampal CA1 pyramidal neurons](https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.1996.76.3.1904) creating distinct computational zones that enable neurons to perform complex integrations of their inputs.

Our quest to understand dendritic computation led us to develop novel experimental approaches. Through pioneering [two-photon microscopy methods](#synaptic-plasticity), we achieved unprecedented views of synaptic function, demonstrating that [synaptic calcium transients in single spines indicate that NMDA receptors are not saturated](https://doi.org/10.1038/20187)—a finding that revolutionized our understanding of synaptic plasticity limits. This technical breakthrough allowed us to bridge our theoretical work on [spike generation and reliability](#spike-reliability) with direct observations of dendritic signaling in living tissue.

Moving beyond single neurons, we explored how dendritic computations shape circuit function in the [olfactory bulb](#olfactory-bulb), where unique dendrodendritic synapses between mitral and granule cells create a specialized processing architecture. Our combined imaging and electrophysiology studies revealed how [dendrodendritic synaptic signals generate both local spine boosts and global low-threshold spikes](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.4746-04.2005), enabling granule cells to perform both local and global computations simultaneously. This work exemplified how dendritic specializations enable neural circuits to implement sophisticated computational strategies, from the precise timing of action potentials to the dynamic regulation of network activity through lateral inhibition.

embodiment.text:
How does the brain give rise to behavior, and how can we decode internal states from the movements and actions we observe? Our research on embodiment explores the deep connections between neural activity, bodily expressions, and cognitive processes. We approach this question through multiple lenses, from theoretical frameworks that bridge ethology and neuroscience to cutting-edge technologies that decode internal states from spontaneous behaviors.

Our journey began with [Big Behavioral Data](#big-behavioral-data), where we explored how the vast streams of behavioral data generated by modern experiments could transform our understanding of brain function. This theoretical work laid the foundation for our empirical investigations by establishing frameworks that connect [ethological observations with neuroscientific inquiry](https://doi.org/10.1101/006809). Building on these insights, we developed methods to decode cognitive variables from the subtle [facial expressions that mice spontaneously produce](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41593-025-02071-5) during decision-making tasks. Through our [Facial Expression Decoding](#face-flipping-decoding) project, we're discovering how internal states like confidence and uncertainty manifest in observable micro-movements of the face, revealing a rich vocabulary of embodied cognition that was previously hidden from view.

Our investigation of embodiment extends to how the brain orchestrates exploratory behaviors that gather information from the environment. In our ongoing work on [Serotonin & Exploratory Movement](#5HT-movement-stefan), we use large-scale electrophysiology to understand how dorsal raphe nucleus serotonin neurons coordinate behaviors like sniffing and whisking. This connects to our parallel investigation of how these same [serotonergic systems encode environmental novelty](#5HT-novelty-stefan) and drive investigatory behavior. We found that phasic activation of these neurons [increases pupil-linked arousal](https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.06.25.171637), suggesting a fundamental role in modulating the animal's engagement with its environment. Together, these projects reveal how cognitive processes are fundamentally embodied phenomena, expressed through and inseparable from the movements and physiological changes that constitute behavior.

learning.text:
How does the brain transform experiences into lasting memories that guide future decisions? Our research into learning and memory has evolved from early investigations of cellular mechanisms to broader questions about how neural circuits support adaptive behavior.

We began by exploring fundamental questions about [hippocampal dendritic computation](#hippo-dendrites), developing computational models to understand how neurons organize their synapses according to Hebbian learning principles. This theoretical work laid the foundation for our experimental investigations into [synaptic plasticity](#synaptic-plasticity), where we pioneered two-photon microscopy methods to visualize calcium dynamics at individual dendritic spines. Our studies revealed critical insights into long-term potentiation (LTP) mechanisms, including the role of AMPA receptors in postsynaptic plasticity ([Mainen et al., 1998](https://doi.org/10.1038/2812)) and the complex dynamics of synaptic transmission ([Hanse & Gustafsson, 2000](https://doi.org/10.1016/s0959-4388(00))).

Building on this cellular foundation, we expanded our focus to understand how memory operates at the circuit and behavioral levels. Our work on [olfactory cortex and memory](#olfactory-memory) investigated how the brain transforms sensory experiences into spatial maps and lasting memories, recording from piriform cortex neurons as animals navigated odor-guided environments. More recently, we have integrated [computational decision theory](#computational-decisions) with our understanding of learning, using Bayesian models combined with reinforcement learning to explain how animals form confidence judgments ([Lak et al., 2019](https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1906787116)) and make optimal decisions under uncertainty. This computational approach has revealed how serotonergic modulation affects learning rates over extended timescales ([Iigaya et al., 2018](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-04840-2)) and how stress influences the inference of task controllability ([Rischewski et al., 2022](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-022-01306-w)), connecting molecular mechanisms to adaptive behavior in complex environments.

neuromodulators.text:
How do neuromodulators like serotonin orchestrate brain-wide activity to shape behavior? This fundamental question has driven our research for nearly two decades, evolving from single-cell recordings to brain-wide investigations of neuromodulatory dynamics. We began by recording from individual serotonin neurons in the dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) while animals performed behavioral tasks, discovering that these cells exhibit [transient firing patterns encoding diverse sensory, motor, and reward events](https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.00507.2009). This [early electrophysiological work](#5ht-electrophysiology) revealed the surprising temporal precision of serotonergic signaling, challenging the classical view of serotonin as a slow, diffuse modulator.

Building on these insights, we developed [optogenetic approaches](#5ht-optogenetics) to causally test how serotonin shapes behavior from sensation to action. By precisely controlling DRN neuron activity with light, we found that [phasic serotonin activation produces complex behavioral effects](https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.20975), including transient locomotor inhibition followed by long-term facilitation. Our investigations expanded to examine how serotonin [gates sensory information flow](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn0609-673), particularly in the olfactory system where [optogenetic DRN stimulation rapidly inhibits spontaneous cortical activity](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.3008-15.2016) while preserving odor responses. We also discovered that serotonin [acutely decreases mechanosensory responsivity](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0105941), suggesting a general role in sensory gating during active behavior.

Our current work leverages cutting-edge technologies to understand neuromodulation at unprecedented scales. Through the [International Brain Laboratory](#ibl), we investigate how neural activity coordinates across hundreds of brain regions during decision-making. Using [Neuropixels recordings combined with optogenetics](#5ht-neuropixels), we track how serotonin orchestrates brain-wide dynamics in real-time. Recent projects explore how serotonin neurons [encode novelty and learning](#5HT-novelty-solene), [signal state prediction errors](#5HT-SPE-felix), and [coordinate exploratory movements](#5HT-movement-stefan) like sniffing and whisking. We've also begun investigating how psychedelics like [LSD alter neural population dynamics](#mouse-lsd) across the brain. By combining

olfaction.text:
How does the brain transform chemical signals into meaningful perceptions that guide behavior? From our earliest work on [olfactory bulb circuitry](#olfactory-bulb), we've sought to understand how the remarkable speed and precision of smell emerges from neural computations. We discovered that [dendrodendritic synapses between mitral and granule cells](#olfactory-bulb) create a dynamic system of lateral inhibition that shapes odor representations through [spike-evoked calcium influx](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.23-20-07551.2003) and [global low-threshold spikes](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.4746-04.2005).

Building on these circuit mechanisms, we asked how animals use olfactory information to make rapid decisions. Our [behavioral studies](#olfactory-decisions) revealed that rats can discriminate odors with [remarkable speed and accuracy](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn1142), completing complex judgments in under 300 milliseconds. We found that [sniffing acts as a fundamental unit of olfactory processing](https://doi.org/10.1093/chemse/bjj016), with animals [precisely controlling their sampling behavior](https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.00071.2007) to optimize information gathering. Moving beyond the bulb, we discovered how the piriform cortex transforms these signals into [distributed rate codes with decorrelated population activity](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2012.04.021), creating representations that support both [spatial navigation and memory formation](#olfactory-memory).

Our understanding of olfaction has extended into unexpected domains. During the COVID-19 pandemic, we rapidly developed [large-scale olfactory screening tests](#covid-olfaction) that helped identify infected individuals through smell loss. Currently, we're exploring how [visual information shapes taste perception](#tasting), revealing systematic associations between color and flavor that demonstrate the profound cross-modal integration in chemosensory processing. Throughout this journey, we've employed diverse approaches—from electrophysiology and calcium imaging to optogenetics and computational modeling—always returning to the fundamental question of how chemical signals in the environment become the rich perceptual experiences that guide our actions.

perception.text:
We seek to understand how organisms transform sensory information into perceptual experiences that guide behavior. This fundamental question drives our investigations across multiple sensory modalities—from smell and touch to vision and taste—examining both the neural mechanisms and computational principles that underlie perception.

Our early work focused on the remarkable perceptual abilities of rodents. In [olfactory decision-making](#olfactory-decisions), we discovered that rats can discriminate odors with extraordinary speed and accuracy, making decisions in as little as 200 milliseconds ([Uchida & Mainen, 2003](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn1142)). We explored the [neural substrates](#superior-colliculus) of rapid sensorimotor decisions in the superior colliculus ([Felsen & Mainen, 2008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2008.09.019)), and investigated how mice actively gather tactile information through their whiskers in the [barrel cortex](#barrel-cortex) ([Sato et al., 2007](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.0050189)). Using optogenetics, we demonstrated that sparse stimulation of barrel cortex neurons could drive learned behaviors ([Houweling & Brecht, 2008](https://doi.org/10.1038/nature06445)), revealing the exquisite sensitivity of the perceptual system.

Building on these neurobiological foundations, we developed [computational frameworks](#computational-decisions) to explain how brains make perceptual decisions under uncertainty. Our theoretical work revealed fundamental limits on deliberation time in perceptual tasks ([Zariwala et al., 2013](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2013.02.010)) and provided mechanistic explanations for classical psychophysical laws ([Pardo-Vazquez et al., 2019](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41593-019-0439-7)). We also advanced probabilistic approaches to understanding how the olfactory system demixes complex odor mixtures ([Mathis et al., 2017](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.4444)). Currently, we are expanding our investigations to human perception, exploring how [visual information shapes taste](#tasting), how [eye movements reveal texture processing](#seeing), and how [confidence emerges in auditory perception](#hearing). We are particularly interested in how [social context](#sharing) modulates sensory experience and how [immersive technologies](#vr-surgery) can alter perceptual states. Through our work on [serotonin and novelty](#5

sensory.text:
How do sensory systems transform physical stimuli into the rich perceptual experiences that guide behavior? We began by exploring this fundamental question in the [olfactory system](#olfactory-bulb), where we discovered how dendrodendritic synapses between mitral and granule cells create lateral inhibition patterns that shape odor processing. Our early work revealed surprising [efficiency and modulation of spike-evoked calcium influx](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.23-20-07551.2003) in these circuits, along with unique [dendrodendritic synaptic signals](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.4746-04.2005) that enable both local and global computations.

This circuit-level understanding led us to investigate how animals use olfactory information to make decisions. We found that rats demonstrate remarkable [speed and accuracy of olfactory discrimination](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn1142), making choices in under 300 milliseconds. Through our [olfactory decision-making](#olfactory-decisions) studies, we discovered that [the sniff acts as a unit of olfactory processing](https://doi.org/10.1093/chemse/bjj016), with animals actively controlling their [rapid and precise sniffing patterns](https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.00071.2007) during discrimination tasks. Moving beyond the bulb, we explored how the [olfactory cortex transforms these sensory signals](#olfactory-memory), revealing [distributed rate coding and decorrelated population activity](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2012.04.021) that supports both perception and memory formation.

Our sensory research has expanded to encompass multiple modalities and their interactions. Through the [International Brain Laboratory](#ibl), we are mapping how sensory evidence transforms into behavioral choices across the entire brain. We study [how gaze patterns reveal texture perception](#seeing), investigate [how visual imagery shapes flavor experience](#tasting), and explore [confidence in auditory perception](#hearing) across different states of consciousness. Even during the COVID-19 pandemic, we applied our olfactory expertise to develop [screening tests](#covid-olfaction) that helped identify infected individuals through smell loss. Across all these projects, we seek to understand not just how sensory systems encode information, but how they create the subjective experiences that guide our interactions with the world.

serotonin.text:
How does serotonin shape behavior from sensory perception to decision-making? We have been investigating this fundamental question for nearly two decades through increasingly sophisticated approaches. Our early [electrophysiological recordings](#5ht-electrophysiology) from serotonin neurons in the dorsal raphe nucleus (DRN) revealed their surprisingly diverse responses, showing [transient firing patterns that encode specific sensory, motor, and reward events](https://doi.org/10.1152/jn.00507.2009). This diversity challenged prevailing views of serotonin as a simple modulatory signal and suggested instead that these neurons carry rich information about ongoing behavior.

Building on these insights, we developed [optogenetic approaches](#5ht-optogenetics) to directly manipulate serotonin neuron activity with millisecond precision during behavior. These experiments demonstrated that [phasic activation of serotonin neurons produces complex behavioral effects](https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.20975), including transient inhibition followed by long-term facilitation of locomotion. We found that serotonin [rapidly inhibits spontaneous neural activity in sensory cortices](https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.3008-15.2016) while leaving stimulus-evoked responses intact, suggesting it acts as a [gate for sensory information flow](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn0609-673). Moreover, serotonin activation [decreases mechanosensory responsivity](https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0105941) and [influences learning rates for rewards over long timescales](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-018-04840-2), revealing its multifaceted role in behavioral control.

Our current work employs cutting-edge [Neuropixels technology](#5ht-neuropixels) to record simultaneously from thousands of neurons across the brain, allowing us to understand how serotonin orchestrates brain-wide neural dynamics. Through the [International Brain Laboratory](#ibl), we are mapping how 279 brain regions coordinate to transform sensory evidence into behavioral choices. We are investigating how [serotonin neurons encode novelty and learning](#5HT-novelty-solene), coordinate [exploratory movements](#5HT-movement-stefan) like sniffing and whisking, and potentially signal [state prediction errors](#5HT-SPE-felix)—mismatches between expected and actual environmental states. Our [functional MRI studies](#serotonin-fmri) complement these approaches by revealing brain-wide patterns of serotonergic modulation. Most

space.text:
How do animals navigate through space using sensory cues, and how does the brain represent spatial goals to guide behavior? We have explored these fundamental questions through the lens of olfaction—a primary sensory modality for rodent navigation—examining how spatial information is encoded across different brain regions during naturalistic behaviors.

Our early work focused on the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC), where we discovered that neurons encode not just immediate sensory information but also [spatial goals during navigation](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2006.06.032). Through our [OFC, Confidence & Decisions](#ofc-confidence) project, we recorded from OFC neurons while rats performed odor-guided spatial tasks, revealing how this region integrates sensory evidence with spatial memory to guide decision-making. Using computational modeling alongside electrophysiology, we showed that OFC activity reflects both the animal's confidence in its spatial decisions and the expected value of different locations, providing a neural substrate for goal-directed navigation.

Building on these insights, we turned to examine how primary sensory areas themselves might encode spatial information. In our [Olfactory Cortex & Memory](#olfactory-memory) project, we made the surprising discovery that piriform cortex—traditionally considered a purely sensory area—contains [spatial maps that emerge during olfactory navigation](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-021-04242-3). By recording from large populations of neurons as mice and rats navigated odor-guided environments, we found that piriform cortex neurons develop place-like responses, creating an allocentric map of space defined by olfactory landmarks. This work revealed how the brain transforms volatile chemical signals into stable spatial representations, demonstrating that even primary sensory cortices participate in the construction of cognitive maps that guide navigation and memory.

spike.text:
We began by asking a fundamental question: how reliable are neurons as computational devices? In the [Spike Generation & Reliability](#spike-reliability) project, we discovered that cortical neurons can generate action potentials with millisecond precision when driven by fluctuating inputs that mimic natural synaptic activity. Using detailed biophysical models validated against intracellular recordings, we showed that this temporal precision emerges from the interaction between voltage-gated ion channels and the statistical properties of synaptic input [Mainen & Sejnowski, 1995](https://doi.org/10.1126/science.7770778).

Our investigations revealed that neuronal morphology plays a crucial role in shaping firing patterns. By systematically varying dendritic structure in our computational models, we demonstrated that the complexity of the dendritic tree influences whether neurons fire regular trains of action potentials or exhibit the irregular, bursty patterns commonly observed in cortical recordings [Mainen & Sejnowski, 1996](https://doi.org/10.1038/382363a0). We also developed a simplified model of spike initiation that captured the essential biophysical mechanisms while remaining computationally tractable [Mainen & Sejnowski, 1995](https://doi.org/10.1016/0896-6273(95)). These findings established that the precise timing of spikes carries information and that neuronal structure fundamentally constrains computational function—principles that continue to inform our understanding of neural coding.

synaptic.text:
How do synapses transform electrical signals into the computational building blocks of neural circuits? Our early work explored this fundamental question through multiple complementary approaches, combining theoretical modeling with cutting-edge experimental techniques to understand synaptic function across different brain systems.

We began by developing [kinetic models](#kinetic-models) that capture the molecular dance of neurotransmitter receptors and ion channels during synaptic transmission. These mathematical frameworks, based on state transition theory, allowed us to [efficiently compute synaptic conductances](https://doi.org/10.1162/neco.1994.6.1.14) and predict how neuromodulators alter synaptic strength. By [synthesizing these models into a common kinetic framework](https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00961734), we created tools that could describe diverse synaptic phenomena—from rapid neurotransmitter-gated currents to slower neuromodulatory effects—using the same underlying principles.

Our theoretical insights guided experimental investigations into synaptic plasticity, the brain's ability to strengthen or weaken connections through experience. We pioneered two-photon microscopy methods to peer into the [hippocampus](#hippo-dendrites) and visualize calcium dynamics at individual dendritic spines during plasticity. These experiments revealed that [NMDA receptors are not saturated](https://doi.org/10.1038/20187) during normal synaptic transmission, providing headroom for synaptic strengthening. We developed new techniques for [estimating calcium concentrations without wavelength ratioing](https://doi.org/10.1016/s0006-3495(00), enabling us to track these crucial plasticity signals more accurately. This work extended to the [olfactory bulb](#olfactory-bulb), where we investigated how specialized dendrodendritic synapses between mitral and granule cells create lateral inhibition patterns that sharpen odor discrimination. Together, these studies established how synaptic mechanisms—from single channel kinetics to spine calcium dynamics—ultimately shape neural computation and sensory processing.

vision.text:
How does the brain transform visual information into behavioral decisions? This fundamental question drives our vision research program, where we explore both the neural mechanisms of visual processing and the active nature of visual perception through eye movements.

Through the [International Brain Laboratory](#ibl), we investigate how the brain coordinates activity across hundreds of regions to convert sensory evidence into behavioral choices. Using cutting-edge Neuropixels recordings, we've created [brain-wide maps of neural activity during complex behavior](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-025-09235-0) and revealed how [prior information is represented across the brain during decision-making](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-025-09226-1). Our collaborative approach has yielded [standardized methods for measuring decision-making in mice](https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.63711), enabling reproducible studies of perceptual processes across laboratories. We've discovered that [mice alternate between discrete strategies during perceptual decision-making](https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.10.19.346353), suggesting that even simple visual decisions involve complex computational processes.

Complementing this neural perspective, our [Gaze and Visual Texture Perception](#seeing) project examines how humans actively explore visual information. We combine high-resolution eye-tracking with behavioral experiments to understand how gaze patterns reveal the underlying mechanisms of texture perception and visual processing. This work bridges microscale neural recordings in mice with macroscale behavioral patterns in humans, providing a comprehensive view of how vision guides behavior across species. Together, these projects illuminate the dynamic interplay between sensory processing, decision-making, and active perception that underlies our visual experience of the world.

volition.text:
How do we decide when to act? This fundamental question about volition and agency has driven our exploration of the neural mechanisms underlying self-initiated actions. Through our [voluntary action project](#voluntary-action), we've investigated how neural circuits generate actions that feel spontaneous and self-determined, seeking to understand what these mechanisms reveal about consciousness and free will.

We began by examining the neural antecedents of self-initiated movements, discovering that [specific patterns of activity in secondary motor cortex predict the timing of voluntary actions](https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.3826). This work revealed that seemingly spontaneous decisions have detectable neural precursors, raising profound questions about the nature of voluntary control. Building on these findings, we identified [distinct deterministic and stochastic components in frontal cortex that together shape action timing decisions](https://doi.org/10.1101/088963), suggesting that volition emerges from an interplay between predictable neural dynamics and inherent variability in brain activity.

Our recent theoretical and computational work has provided a mechanistic framework for understanding these phenomena. We demonstrated that [metastable neural attractors can explain the variable timing of stable behavioral sequences](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2021.10.011), offering a circuit-level explanation for how the brain balances flexibility with reliability in voluntary action control. This research program, spanning empirical studies in both humans and rats alongside computational modeling, has led us to [reconsider traditional philosophical questions about free will through the lens of modern neuroscience](https://doi.org/10.1101/sqb.2014.79.024810). We continue to explore how [cortical circuit mechanisms prepare and select actions](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.conb.2015.01.005), seeking to bridge the gap between neural mechanisms and our subjective experience of agency.
